title: Closeouts: Stop the Shot, Contain the Drive

lesson.title: How to close out without getting blown by
lesson.body_md:
## Why closeouts matter

A closeout is the sprint from a help spot back to your shooter when the ball is skipped or swung. If you do it wrong, two bad things happen: the shooter gets a clean look, **or** they rip past you for a layup. Good closeouts take away both.

## The three jobs of a closeout

1. **Get there.** Sprint the first two-thirds of the distance.
2. **Break down.** Choppy, short steps the last third with your hands up high.
3. **Take something away.** You can't take away the shot **and** the drive. Pick one based on the shooter.

## Read the shooter

- **Shooter first?** Closeout high, hand up, force a drive into help.
- **Driver first?** Closeout short. Stay low. Force a tougher pull-up.
- **Both?** Lean to take away the strong hand. Trust your help defender behind you.

## The 5 rules

1. **Sprint then chop.** Long strides early, short steps late. Never run in a straight line all the way to the shooter.
2. **High hand on the shooting side.** Don't slap. Just contest with the inside hand up.
3. **Inside foot up.** The foot closer to the middle is forward. This shades the drive away from the rim.
4. **Don't fly past.** A bad closeout is worse than no closeout. If you can't break down in time, stay home.
5. **Talk on the way.** "Closing!" tells the help defender you've got it.

## Common mistakes

- **Running upright.** You'll skid past the shooter every time. Sink your hips.
- **Two hands down.** That's a wide open shot.
- **Closing out flat.** Square shoulders means the shooter picks any direction. Angle your hips so they only have one good option.
- **Late and lazy.** A closeout that arrives after the ball goes up is just standing under a jump shot.

## What you're training

The Scenario Engine will put you in moments where the ball is in the air to a shooter, and you have a half-second to pick the right closeout angle. You're training your eyes to read **shooter or driver** before your feet move. That recognition is what separates good defenders from great ones.

## Coach's cue

> *"Sprint, chop, contest. Never both feet flat. Never both hands down."*

When you see the skip pass leave the passer's hands, your first step should already be moving. Don't wait for the catch.
